title: Performance Basics
intro: Your task manager is live and people are using it. But some pages load slowly and users are bouncing. Let's learn what makes web apps fast — and what slows them down.

teach.title: Why Performance Matters
teach.body: Users expect pages to load in under 2 seconds. Every extra second of load time increases bounce rates dramatically. Performance isn't just about speed — it's about user experience, SEO rankings, and even conversion rates. A fast app feels professional and trustworthy.

teach.title: Performance Is Like a Fast-Food Restaurant
teach.body: Nobody wants to wait 20 minutes for a burger. Fast-food restaurants optimize everything: pre-heat the grill, prep ingredients in advance, and have a streamlined workflow. Your app should do the same: pre-load data, cache common requests, and minimize unnecessary work.
The biggest performance wins usually come from three things: reducing the amount of JavaScript you ship, optimizing images, and caching data that doesn't change often.

cards.content: Lazy load components that aren't visible on the initial screen to reduce the initial bundle size
cards.explanation: Code splitting and lazy loading mean users only download the JavaScript they need right now. A task manager doesn't need to load the settings page code until the user navigates there.

cards.content: Load all your data on the client side after the page renders to keep the server response fast
cards.explanation: Client-side data fetching causes a loading spinner after the page appears. Server-side rendering or static generation sends the data with the HTML, so the page is usable immediately.

cards.content: Optimize images by using modern formats like WebP and serving appropriately sized versions
cards.explanation: Images are often the heaviest assets on a page. Using WebP instead of PNG can reduce file size by 30-50%. Next.js's Image component does this automatically.

cards.content: Premature optimization — spending time optimizing code that's already fast enough — is a waste of time
cards.explanation: Don't optimize blindly. Measure first with tools like Lighthouse or Chrome DevTools. Fix the actual bottlenecks, not what you guess might be slow.

cards.content: Install as many npm packages as needed — the more features your app has, the better the user experience
cards.explanation: Every package adds to your bundle size. A date picker library might add 50KB. Evaluate whether you really need each dependency or if a simpler solution exists.

cards.content: Use a Content Delivery Network (CDN) to serve static assets from servers close to your users
cards.explanation: CDNs cache your static files (images, CSS, JS) on servers worldwide. A user in Tokyo gets files from a Tokyo server instead of waiting for a response from New York. Vercel does this automatically.
